text: The surgical team drapes the patient widely to maximize space on the abdominal wall for port placement, ensuring adequate room to prevent collisions between the robotic arms during the procedure.

The surgeon inserts an optical blunt tip trocar through the umbilicus under direct laparoscopic visualization, safely entering the abdominal cavity. The peritoneal space is inspected to identify target anatomy and check for adhesions or obstacles before planning port sites.

The surgeon places the first port in the very lateral right upper quadrant, entering horizontally above the colon in the right gutter to maximize space and avoid collisions. The next port is positioned in the midclavicular line of the right upper quadrant, situated halfway between the camera port and the lateral port, and is more superior than the lateral port.

The surgeon places the left upper quadrant port with a side port for smoke irrigation, positioned more laterally and inferiorly than typical, to prevent interference from the falciform ligament during instrument insertion.

The patient is positioned with the table in its lowest position, angled fifteen to twenty degrees in reverse Trendelenburg and slightly turned to the left. The robotic system is docked over the patient's right shoulder, aligning the camera port, gallbladder, and robotic column in a straight line.

The surgeon attaches the camera arm to the trocar at the umbilicus, followed by the placement of the left upper quadrant arm at its designated port site. The process is repeated for arm number three in the lateral right upper quadrant and the midclavicular right upper quadrant arm. Either the midclavicular or lateral right upper quadrant port can be left undocked for use as an assistant port.

The robotic arms are positioned with significant distance between them to prevent collisions. The surgeon inspects the abdominal ports to ensure the remote centers are correctly positioned within the muscle, followed by the insertion of instruments under direct visualization.

The surgeon begins dissection by lysing adhesions to the intra-abdominal wall using a harmonic scalpel in arm number one on the right side of the screen. Extensive adhesions of the omentum to the gallbladder are cleared to identify gallbladder anatomy. On the left side of the screen, arm number two uses a fenestrated bipolar device for grasping and retracting, providing low grip strength to avoid injuring friable tissues and assisting in hemostasis with bipolar cautery.

The surgeon uses a suction irrigation device to manage bile spillage and oozing from the friable gallbladder. The Maryland dissector is employed for precise dissection around the cystic arteries, utilizing its wrist action and fine tips. A branch of the cystic artery is identified and clipped with Hemlock clips before being divided with the harmonic scalpel. The suction irrigator is used to maintain a clear visual field and assist in blunt tissue manipulation.

The surgeon continues to dissect down the front of the gallbladder, clearing additional adhesions and working toward the base. Hartmann's pouch becomes visible despite the distorted anatomy due to acute inflammation. The suction irrigator is used for blunt dissection, and a second branch of the cystic artery is identified and clipped for hemostasis, staying high on the gallbladder wall to avoid injury to structures at the base.

The surgeon performs a modified top-down dissection of the gallbladder, starting in the middle of the gallbladder bed and using a harmonic scalpel to separate the gallbladder from the liver bed. The posterior wall of the gallbladder is dissected to create a window behind the liver, aided by a fan retractor placed through the lateral right upper quadrant arm. This retraction improves visualization of the anatomy at the base of the gallbladder. The surgeon continues to dissect fatty tissue and adhesions, clearly identifying Hartman's pouch and the cystic duct. The cystic duct is skeletonized from surrounding scar tissue, clipped, and divided safely, avoiding injury to nearby structures.

The surgeon uses monopolar cautery scissors to dissect the remaining portion of the gallbladder from the liver bed, working superiorly along the posterior wall while maintaining hemostasis. A portion of the gallbladder is left attached to the liver for retraction purposes, exposing the liver bed. A small posterior branch is identified as bleeding, and a hemostatic clip is applied to control the bleeding.

The robotic platform provides enhanced visualization, allowing the surgeon to identify small vessels and apply clips or bipolar cautery for hemostasis. The suction irrigator is used to aspirate blood and fluids, ensuring thorough inspection for bleeding. A starch-based Rista Coagulant is applied to the friable liver bed to prevent postoperative bleeding. The procedure concludes with the division of the remaining attachments of the gallbladder to the liver.

In this case, we were able to complete a very difficult, complex cholecystectomy that would be very difficult to do with either single-site, SILs, or even standard four-point laparoscopy. And we're significantly aided by not only the HD enhanced vision, but also the specialized instruments, such as the suction irrigator, the articulating angled dissectors, the fan retractor, harmonic scalpel, bipolar cautery, and a variety of graspers that are all available through the multi-site da Vinci platform.
